key_departures.impact: Four-time Pro Bowl edge rusher and the team's best pass rusher. Was limited to 7 games in 2025 due to core muscle and hip injuries but led the league with 17.5 sacks in both 2023 and 2024. Bengals declined the franchise tag (worth $24.434M) after a prolonged contract dispute. The relationship had fully frayed. His departure removes the one elite pass-rush presence on the defense.

Signed a 3-year, $36M deal with the Jets ($22.5M fully guaranteed). Ossai had back-to-back 5-sack seasons as a rotational rusher and was one of Cincinnati's most effective young pass rushers. Losing him alongside Hendrickson strips the Bengals of both EDGE starters entering 2026.

Stone started for the Bengals but never fully met expectations. Had a career-high 104 tackles in 2025 and 13 interceptions over his last three seasons, but the team actively replaced him with Bryan Cook, signaling they viewed him as an upgrade target rather than a keeper.

Signed a 2-year deal with the Saints. Fant served as Cincinnati's TE2 behind Mike Gesicki, posting 34 catches for 288 yards and 3 TDs in 2025, but notoriously lost 3 fumbles returned for touchdowns. His departure is a depth loss — Mike Gesicki remains — but the Bengals still lack a legitimate TE2.

Signed a 1-year, $4.215M deal (max value) with the Titans. Volson was a three-year interior starter who was benched late in 2024 and missed all of 2025 with shoulder surgery. His departure is net-neutral — he was not expected to start in 2026.

Bengals declined to tender Browning at any RFA level following poor performance filling in for Burrow in 2025. The team is open to a reunion at the minimum. Departure creates a backup QB need behind Joe Burrow.
Was a declining starter. The Bengals were already expected to address CB in the draft or FA regardless of his status. His departure leaves a hole at CB2 alongside Josh Newton.

Sought a starting role in free agency. Flacco made 6 starts with the Bengals in 2025 after being acquired mid-season from Cleveland. His departure compounds the backup QB need, though the team may still reunite with him if he can't find a starting job.
Rotational edge rusher. Losing Sample further thins an already depleted pass-rush group.
Depth/special teams IOL. Played just 23 snaps in 2025. Minimal impact.
Depth CB. No meaningful snaps in 2025.
Depth safety. Replaced by Cook acquisition.

signings.analysis: Three-year, $60M deal at $20M AAV — solidly in the starter tier for EDGE. Mafe, 27, is a Super Bowl champion who ranked 8th in the NFL in pass-rush win rate (EDGE8) in 2025 despite posting only 2 sacks, with a 12.4% pressure rate. He had 9 sacks in 2023 and 6 in 2024 before a reduced rotational role in Seattle's deep unit. The Bengals paid a premium vs. market projection (~$12M/yr) believing he'll bounce back with a full-time starting role. However, he's not a replacement for elite-tier Trey Hendrickson (who went for likely $28M+/yr). The 3-year term signals real investment, but Mafe's 2-sack 2025 creates skepticism. EDGE remains a need — the Bengals must still draft a EDGE in rounds 1-3 to pair alongside Mafe and develop young players Shemar Stewart and Myles Murphy. Need drops from Tier 1 critical to Tier 2.

Three-year, $40.25M deal ($18M in Year 1) for the top safety on the 2026 market. Cook, 25, earned an 83.5 PFF grade in 2025 (career high) and ranked 4th-lowest in missed tackle rate among safeties with 750+ defensive snaps. He gives Cincinnati a proven, high-floor starting safety to pair alongside Jordan Battle — a position that has been a revolving door since Jessie Bates III left in 2023. Contract has $14M guaranteed per OverTheCap, which is relatively modest for the total value, but $18M in Year 1 provides real commitment. $13.4M AAV is squarely in the starter tier for S. S need is fully addressed. No need to draft a safety in early rounds.

One-year deal at up to $5M to retain their starting right guard. Risner was a major addition last offseason and helped stabilize a much-improved offensive line in 2025. His re-signing keeps the IOL from being a top priority but the 1-year structure means it's a bridge, not a long-term solution. The team lost Cordell Volson (who missed all of 2025) and Lucas Patrick, but Risner holding the RG spot means IOL is a moderate need, not critical. Center depth and a long-term RG succession plan remain on the agenda.

One-year extension for the Bengals' nickel back who played nearly as many snaps in 2025 as in his previous five seasons combined. Depth/slot CB re-sign at below-starter money. Does not solve the CB2 need created by Taylor-Britt's departure. CB remains a legitimate draft need.
One-year minimum re-sign for the backup RB. Maintains depth behind Samaje Perine. RB was not a pre-FA need and this doesn't change the picture.
One-year minimum re-sign for a depth WR. No impact on needs. Cincinnati already has Ja'Marr Chase, Tee Higgins, and Andrei Iosivas at the position.

One-year extension for a depth TE. Provides minimal cap-level TE2 insurance with Noah Fant departing, but Hudson is not a starter-caliber solution. TE remains a need, particularly after Fant's departure to New Orleans.
One-year minimum extension for a depth WR. No need impact.
One-year minimum extension for a depth/special teams safety. S need is already fully addressed by Cook signing.
One-year minimum for a special teams/depth LB. LB remains a need at the starter level.
One-year minimum for a developmental/depth TE. Does not address the TE need created by Fant's departure.
One-year minimum for a depth/special teams LB. No impact on starter-level LB need.
One-year minimum for a developmental EDGE. Provides depth behind Mafe, Shemar Stewart, and Myles Murphy but does not address the starter-level EDGE need beyond Mafe.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Bengals lost both Hendrickson (elite, 4x Pro Bowl) and Ossai (rotational, 5 sacks) in free agency, stripping the entire pass-rush corps. Mafe signing (3yr/$60M, $20M AAV) partially addresses this — he's a legitimate starter with an elite pressure rate — but his 2-sack 2025 season and the scale of the departure means EDGE is still a top-2 draft priority. Shemar Stewart (2025 R1) and Myles Murphy need a veteran pairing/mentor. Should target an EDGE in rounds 1-3 of the 2026 draft.

Bryan Cook signing (3yr/$40.25M, $18M Year 1) fully addresses S. Cook is the top safety on the 2026 market, graded 83.5 PFF in 2025 with 4th-lowest missed tackle rate at the position. He pairs with Jordan Battle for a legitimate starting duo. S is no longer a draft need — can wait for Day 3 depth at best.

No IDL move made in free agency. The Bengals finished dead last against the run in 2025 and allowed 2,500 rushing yards. Kris Jenkins Jr. is the best internal candidate to emerge but the team cannot rely on that projection alone. IDL remains a top-3 draft need — should target an interior lineman in rounds 1-3.

No LB signed above minimum level. The Bengals started two rookie LBs (Demetrius Knight, Barrett Carter) in 2025 to poor results, and Logan Wilson was traded mid-season. Devin Lloyd went to Panthers over Bengals in a late Day 1 decision. LB remains a genuine need — a veteran starter or high-upside draftee must be added.

Cam Taylor-Britt departure escalates CB need. Re-signing Jalen Davis (nickel, 1yr/~$3M) provides slot depth only. Josh Newton is the projected CB1 but a starting-caliber CB2 opposite him is now critical. No substantive CB addition has been made. CB should be targeted in rounds 2-4 of the draft.

Dalton Risner re-signing (1yr/$5M) retains a proven starting RG and prevents IOL from being an emergency need. However, the 1-year structure means a long-term answer is still needed. Volson is gone, Patrick is gone. IOL remains a day 2-3 draft consideration for succession planning, but Risner bridge reduces urgency from critical to moderate.

Browning not tendered, Flacco in free agency seeking a starting role. Bengals have no backup QB under contract with Joe Burrow missing 9 games in 2025. Given Burrow's injury history, a competent backup is mandatory. Team needs to add a QB2 either via FA (Flacco reunion likely) or late-round draft pick.

Noah Fant departed to New Orleans. Mike Gesicki (TE1) remains, but Tanner Hudson (depth/minimum) is the only TE2 option. Fant's departure creates a clear TE2 hole. The team should look to address this in rounds 3-5 of the draft or mid-level free agency.

No change. Orlando Brown Jr. (LT) remains and the team views the OT room as stable short-term, though Brown's age (28 but injury history) keeps long-term succession on the radar. No moves made or needed here urgently.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion pass rusher signed to replace Hendrickson and anchor a rebuilt defensive front
Top safety on the 2026 market returns to his hometown Cincinnati after four championship years in Kansas City
Proven starting RG retained on bridge deal to anchor the right side of the offensive line
Slot corner re-signed after breakout season to maintain nickel depth

key_losses.headline: Four-time Pro Bowler and two-time 17.5-sack leader departs after Bengals declined franchise tag, ending a prolonged contract saga
Rising 25-year-old pass rusher (5 sacks, career-best pressure rate) walks to the Jets, stripping the Bengals of both EDGE starters
TE2 departs after one season in Cincinnati, leaving Gesicki without a capable complementary tight end
Declining starter departs, leaving a CB2 void opposite Josh Newton heading into the draft
Backup QB not tendered, leaving Bengals without a capable QB2 behind injury-prone Joe Burrow

notes: All signings reported via credible insiders (Schefter, Rapoport, Pelissero) during the legal tampering window (March 9, 2026). Contracts cannot be made official until the new league year opens March 11, 2026. Bryan Cook contract guaranteed figure ($14M) sourced from OverTheCap. Boye Mafe guaranteed figure (~$28M) is estimated based on market context — exact guarantee not officially reported as of March 10, 2026. Trey Hendrickson's new team is TBD; Cowboys were interested but pivoted to Rashan Gary trade; Colts and Commanders also linked. Hendrickson departure is confirmed. All minimum/depth re-signing contract values are estimates based on market and cap context — official terms not publicly reported for several of the January/February reserve futures signings.
